questions.question_text: An affiliated agent fails to make a required disclosure, and the injured buyer sues the supervising broker for negligent supervision. Which fact would MOST strongly support holding the broker liable?
questions.correct_answer: The broker had no written office policies, provided no training, and never reviewed transaction files
distractors: The broker simply employed the agent at the time of the violation
The broker required annual compliance training and reviewed every transaction file
The agent was classified as an independent contractor rather than a W-2 employee
questions.explanation: Negligent supervision turns on the adequacy of the broker's supervisory systems, not on the mere fact of employment. A broker with no written policies, no training program, and no transaction review has failed the affirmative duty license law imposes to supervise affiliated licensees — the classic negligent-supervision fact pattern. Merely employing the agent is not itself negligence; evidence of regular training and file review cuts against liability rather than supporting it; and independent contractor status affects tax treatment, not license-law supervisory duties, which cannot be contracted away.

questions.question_text: Several competing real estate brokers in a market agree to charge the same commission rate to avoid 'price wars.' This arrangement is:
questions.correct_answer: Illegal price-fixing under federal antitrust law
distractors: Acceptable as long as the agreement is informal and unwritten
Permitted if the brokers represent different market segments
Legal if the agreed rate reflects the prevailing market rate
questions.explanation: Price-fixing — competitors agreeing to charge the same price — is a per se violation of the Sherman Antitrust Act, meaning it is illegal regardless of how reasonable the agreed rate seems or whether it harms consumers. Real estate commissions are always negotiable between the individual broker and their client. Brokers who discuss commission rates with competitors risk criminal prosecution (fines and imprisonment), civil suits, and license discipline. Other antitrust violations common in real estate: market allocation (agreeing to divide territories) and group boycotts (agreeing not to cooperate with certain brokers or companies).

questions.question_text: A group of brokers agrees not to show or cooperate with a new discount brokerage that has entered their market. This is an example of:
questions.correct_answer: A group boycott
distractors: Market allocation
A tie-in arrangement
Price-fixing
questions.explanation: A group boycott (also called a concerted refusal to deal) is an agreement among competitors to collectively refuse to do business with a third party. In real estate, brokers agreeing to boycott a discount competitor — refusing to show their listings or cooperate on transactions — is a per se antitrust violation. Each broker is individually free to decide not to cooperate with any particular brokerage, but an agreement among competitors to do so collectively is illegal. Market allocation involves dividing geographic areas or client types. Tie-in arrangements condition a service on purchasing another service.

questions.question_text: A listing agent tells a buyer that the home has 'never had any water issues' when they know the basement floods annually. This is:
questions.correct_answer: Fraudulent misrepresentation
distractors: Negligent misrepresentation
Puffery
A permissible omission
questions.explanation: Fraudulent misrepresentation requires: (1) a false statement of material fact, (2) knowledge that it is false (scienter), (3) intent to induce reliance, (4) justifiable reliance by the other party, and (5) resulting damage. Here, all elements are present. Negligent misrepresentation occurs when an agent makes a false statement without knowing it is false but fails to exercise reasonable care to verify the truth (e.g., stating square footage from memory without checking). Puffery (subjective opinions like 'great neighborhood') is not actionable. Agents have proactive disclosure obligations — they cannot avoid liability by staying silent about known material defects.

questions.question_text: Errors and omissions (E&O) insurance is designed to protect real estate licensees from:
questions.correct_answer: Claims arising from unintentional professional mistakes
distractors: Claims arising from intentional fraud or willful law violations
Physical injuries occurring during a showing or open house
Losses from market value declines after a client purchases
questions.explanation: E&O insurance (professional liability insurance) covers claims arising from unintentional professional errors — missed disclosures, incorrect information, failure to follow client instructions, overlooked contract deadlines, and similar mistakes. It does not cover intentional wrongdoing, criminal acts, fraud, or willful violations of law (including fair housing violations). Physical injury at a property is covered by general liability or the property owner's insurance, not E&O. Market value declines are investment risks, not professional errors. E&O is required in many states for licensees and/or brokerages.

questions.question_text: An agent wants to call potential seller leads from a list of phone numbers they purchased. Before calling, the agent MUST:
questions.correct_answer: Check the numbers against the National Do Not Call Registry
distractors: Obtain written consent from each person on the list first
Register each call with the FTC within 24 hours
No requirements apply to purchased lead lists
questions.explanation: The FTC's National Do Not Call Registry prohibits telemarketers — including real estate agents making unsolicited sales calls — from calling numbers registered on the list. Exceptions include: prior established business relationships (within 18 months of a transaction or 3 months of an inquiry), calls to businesses, and calls where the consumer has given prior written consent. Agents must scrub their call lists against the registry before calling. Violations can result in civil penalties currently exceeding $50,000 per violation (adjusted annually for inflation). Real estate agents are not exempt — the rule applies to anyone making sales solicitation calls.

questions.question_text: Under the doctrine of respondeat superior, a broker may be liable for an agent's wrongful acts when:
questions.correct_answer: The agent acted within the scope of employment or authorized activities
distractors: The broker personally witnessed the agent's wrongful act
The agent was classified as a W-2 employee, not an independent contractor
The broker received a direct financial benefit from the wrongful act
questions.explanation: Respondeat superior ('let the master answer') holds employers liable for employees' wrongful acts committed within the scope of their employment. In real estate, this applies regardless of independent contractor status — license law imposes supervisory duties that cannot be contracted away. The broker does not need to witness the act, benefit from it, or have the agent classified as W-2. If the agent was performing real estate activities under the broker's license, the broker may be vicariously liable.

questions.question_text: A broker can BEST minimize liability for agents' errors by:
questions.correct_answer: Implementing written policies, regular training, and active transaction review procedures
distractors: Requiring all agents to sign waivers of liability releasing the broker from responsibility
Classifying all agents as independent contractors to eliminate supervisory obligations
Avoiding any knowledge of agent activities so the broker cannot be accused of awareness
questions.explanation: Proactive supervision is the broker's best defense. Written policies establish expectations, regular training ensures agents understand their obligations, and transaction review catches errors before they become costly claims. Liability waivers between broker and agent do not protect the broker from third-party claims. Independent contractor status does not eliminate license law supervisory duties. Deliberate ignorance ('willful blindness') can actually increase liability — courts may find the broker negligent for failing to supervise.

questions.question_text: A salesperson acting outside the scope of their broker's authorization commits fraud against a buyer. The broker:
questions.correct_answer: May still face liability for failing to implement reasonable supervision
distractors: Has no liability because the agent acted beyond authorized scope
Is cleared if they show the agent was an independent contractor
Is liable only if they specifically instructed the agent to commit fraud
questions.explanation: Even when an agent acts outside their authority, brokers can face liability for negligent supervision. If the broker failed to implement reasonable policies, training, or oversight that could have prevented or detected the fraudulent conduct, they may be liable for negligence. Courts examine whether the broker's supervisory systems were adequate — not just whether the specific act was authorized. A broker who has no review procedures, no training, and no oversight is more likely to be found negligently responsible.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is a broker's supervisory responsibility under license law?
questions.correct_answer: Reviewing transaction files and ensuring license law compliance
distractors: Setting each agent's daily work schedule and office hours
Personally attending every showing and open house
Approving each agent's social media posts before publishing
questions.explanation: Brokers must supervise agents' licensed activities — reviewing transaction files, ensuring required disclosures are made, monitoring trust account handling, and verifying compliance with license law. This does not mean micromanaging every aspect of an agent's daily activities. Setting work hours and requiring office attendance relates to employment classification (employee vs. independent contractor), not license law supervision. Brokers should have systems to review key documents, not attend every showing or approve every social media post.

questions.question_text: Market allocation in real estate antitrust law refers to:
questions.correct_answer: Competitors agreeing to divide territories or customer types
distractors: A broker assigning agents to specialize in different neighborhoods
The natural division of a market into residential and commercial segments
A franchisor requiring franchisees to operate in specific territories
questions.explanation: Market allocation occurs when competing brokers agree to divide geographic areas or customer types among themselves to eliminate competition. For example, 'you take the east side, I'll take the west side' or 'you handle commercial, I'll handle residential.' This is a per se antitrust violation. It is different from a broker internally assigning agents to specialize (which is a management decision within one company) or a franchisor setting franchise territories (which is a vertical, not horizontal, arrangement).

questions.question_text: A tie-in arrangement (tying agreement) in real estate occurs when:
questions.correct_answer: A provider requires use of another service as a condition of the deal
distractors: Two competing brokers agree to charge identical commission rates
A broker ties their commission to the final sale price
An agent requires buyer and seller to split closing costs equally
questions.explanation: A tie-in arrangement conditions the purchase of one product or service on the purchase of another. In real estate, examples include: a broker requiring clients to use a specific title company, mortgage lender, or home warranty company as a condition of representation. While agents can recommend service providers, requiring their use as a condition of the agency relationship is an antitrust violation. RESPA also prohibits kickbacks between settlement service providers, which often accompanies tie-in arrangements.

questions.question_text: An agent tells a seller: 'All agents in this area charge 6% — that's the standard rate.' This statement:
questions.correct_answer: Is false and potentially an antitrust violation
distractors: Is accurate because local boards set standard commission rates
Is permitted if the agent's brokerage actually charges 6%
Is required so sellers know what to expect in the market
questions.explanation: There is no 'standard' commission rate. Telling a seller that all agents charge the same rate implies a price-fixing agreement among competitors, which is an antitrust violation. Even if many agents in an area happen to charge similar rates, each broker sets their own fees independently. Agents should present their own commission rate and explain it on its merits — never reference what competitors charge as evidence of a 'standard.' NAR and all real estate boards explicitly prohibit establishing or enforcing standard commission rates.

questions.question_text: The difference between fraudulent misrepresentation and negligent misrepresentation is:
questions.correct_answer: Fraudulent requires knowing the statement is false; negligent does not
distractors: Fraudulent involves property defects; negligent involves financial matters
Negligent carries criminal penalties; fraudulent carries only civil penalties
Fraudulent must be in writing; negligent can be oral or written
questions.explanation: The key distinction is intent (scienter). Fraudulent misrepresentation requires the speaker to know the statement is false (or to make it with reckless disregard for the truth). Negligent misrepresentation occurs when the speaker should have known the statement was false through reasonable diligence but failed to verify. Both can result in civil liability. Fraud may also carry criminal penalties. The form of the statement (oral or written) and the subject matter do not distinguish the two — the agent's state of mind does.

questions.question_text: An agent describes a property as being in 'the best neighborhood in town.' This is an example of:
questions.correct_answer: Puffery
distractors: Fraudulent misrepresentation
Negligent misrepresentation
A material misstatement
questions.explanation: Puffery (puffing) consists of general, subjective statements of opinion that no reasonable person would take as statements of fact — 'beautiful home,' 'great investment,' 'best location.' These are not actionable as misrepresentation because they are understood as sales enthusiasm, not factual claims. The line between puffery and misrepresentation: if the statement can be verified or disproved as a fact, it may be misrepresentation. 'Best neighborhood' is subjective opinion; 'property values here increased 20% last year' is a factual claim that must be accurate.

questions.question_text: An agent tells a buyer the home has 2,400 square feet based on the previous listing without measuring. The actual size is 1,900 square feet. This is MOST likely:
questions.correct_answer: Negligent misrepresentation
distractors: Puffery
No violation
Fraudulent misrepresentation
questions.explanation: This is negligent misrepresentation because the agent stated the square footage as fact without reasonable verification. Square footage is a material fact — not puffery — and agents have a duty to verify factual claims they make about properties. Relying on a prior listing without independent verification falls below the standard of reasonable care. It is not fraud because the agent did not know the information was false (no intent to deceive). Agents should measure or verify with official records before quoting square footage.

questions.question_text: An agent who actively conceals a known defect by covering it before showings commits:
questions.correct_answer: Fraud
distractors: Negligent misrepresentation
Puffery
No violation
questions.explanation: Active concealment goes beyond mere silence (passive nondisclosure) — it involves deliberate steps to hide a material defect from discovery. Covering water stains, painting over mold, or placing furniture over damaged flooring demonstrates intent to deceive, which elevates the conduct from negligence to fraud. Fraud through active concealment exposes the agent to criminal prosecution, civil liability for damages, and license revocation. Normal staging and property preparation is acceptable; concealing known defects is not.

questions.question_text: E&O insurance typically does NOT cover claims arising from:
questions.correct_answer: Intentional fraud, criminal acts, or knowing violations of law
distractors: Missed contract deadlines that caused a buyer to lose the property
Incorrect property information provided in good faith
Failure to disclose a fact the agent should have discovered
questions.explanation: E&O insurance covers unintentional professional errors — mistakes made in good faith while performing licensed activities. It does not cover intentional wrongdoing: fraud, deliberate misrepresentation, criminal conduct, discrimination, or knowing violations of law or regulation. The rationale is that insurance should not incentivize bad behavior by covering its consequences. Missed deadlines, incorrect information given in good faith, and negligent failure to discover facts are exactly the types of errors E&O is designed to cover.

questions.question_text: A claims-made E&O policy covers claims that are:
questions.correct_answer: Filed during the policy period
distractors: Based on errors that occurred during the policy period
Filed within 30 days of the error being discovered
Made only against the named insured, not affiliated agents
questions.explanation: Claims-made policies cover claims filed during the active policy period. If an agent makes an error in Year 1 but the client doesn't discover it and file a claim until Year 3, the Year 3 policy responds (assuming coverage was continuous). An occurrence-based policy would instead cover the Year 1 policy since that's when the error occurred. Most E&O policies are claims-made, which is why maintaining continuous coverage is important — gaps in coverage could leave agents exposed to claims from past transactions.

questions.question_text: Which of the following scenarios would MOST likely be covered by an agent's E&O insurance?
questions.correct_answer: The agent forgot to include a contingency clause the buyer requested
distractors: The agent knowingly lied about property condition to induce a purchase
The agent embezzled earnest money from the brokerage trust account
The agent refused to show homes in a neighborhood based on race
questions.explanation: Forgetting to include a requested contingency is a classic professional error — an unintentional mistake in the course of performing licensed activities. This is exactly what E&O insurance covers. Lying about property condition is intentional fraud, embezzlement is a criminal act, and racial steering is a knowing fair housing violation — none of these are covered by E&O policies because they involve intentional wrongdoing, not professional negligence.

questions.question_text: Some states require all real estate licensees to carry E&O insurance. The primary purpose of this requirement is to:
questions.correct_answer: Ensure consumers can recover damages from a licensee's professional errors
distractors: Generate premium revenue for the state's real estate recovery fund
Prevent agents from filing bankruptcy to avoid liability claims
Provide health insurance benefits to independent contractor agents
questions.explanation: Mandatory E&O insurance protects consumers by ensuring that if a licensee makes a professional error that causes financial harm, there is an insurance policy to pay the damages. Without E&O, a judgment against an agent with limited personal assets might be uncollectible, leaving the consumer with no recovery. This is similar to why states require auto insurance — to ensure victims can be compensated. E&O is separate from the state recovery fund, which covers claims where insurance and personal assets are insufficient.

questions.question_text: The established business relationship exemption to the Do Not Call rules allows an agent to call:
questions.correct_answer: A former client for up to 18 months after the last transaction
distractors: Anyone who has visited the agent's website or attended an open house
Former clients indefinitely if the agent maintains their contact info
Anyone referred by a current client regardless of DNC registration
questions.explanation: The FTC's established business relationship (EBR) exemption allows calls to: (1) existing customers for up to 18 months after the last transaction, and (2) anyone who made an inquiry for up to 3 months after the inquiry. After these periods expire, the numbers must be treated as any other — checked against the Do Not Call Registry. Simply visiting a website or attending an open house may create a 3-month inquiry window, but not an 18-month transaction relationship. Referrals do not create an EBR with the referred person.

questions.question_text: The CAN-SPAM Act applies to real estate agents who send commercial emails by requiring:
questions.correct_answer: A clear opt-out mechanism, accurate sender info, and honest subject lines
distractors: Prior written consent from every recipient before sending any email
Registration with the FTC and payment of a per-email fee
Encryption of all emails containing property listing information
questions.explanation: The CAN-SPAM Act (2003) regulates commercial email by requiring: (1) accurate 'From' and 'Reply-To' information, (2) truthful subject lines, (3) identification as an advertisement, (4) the sender's valid physical postal address, (5) a clear opt-out mechanism, and (6) honoring opt-out requests within 10 business days. Unlike the Do Not Call Registry, CAN-SPAM does not require prior consent to send the first email — it regulates the content and provides an opt-out right. Violations can result in civil penalties currently exceeding $50,000 per violation (adjusted annually for inflation).

questions.question_text: The Telephone Consumer Protection Act (TCPA) restricts real estate agents from:
questions.correct_answer: Sending unsolicited texts or robocalls without prior written consent
distractors: Making any phone calls to prospective clients during business hours
Discussing commission rates over the phone instead of in writing
Using a personal cell phone number for business calls
questions.explanation: The TCPA prohibits autodialed calls, prerecorded messages, and unsolicited text messages to cell phones without prior express written consent. For real estate agents, this means they cannot send mass text blasts, use auto-dialers, or send automated messages to people who haven't opted in. Manual one-to-one calls are generally permitted (subject to Do Not Call rules), but automated or mass communications require consent. TCPA violations can result in damages of $500-$1,500 per unauthorized call or text.

questions.question_text: An agent maintains their own 'internal do not call' list of people who have asked not to be called. This list is:
questions.correct_answer: Required by federal law
distractors: Optional but recommended as a best practice
Required only for brokerages with more than five agents
Unnecessary if calls are made during permitted hours only
questions.explanation: Federal telemarketing rules require two types of compliance: (1) checking the National Do Not Call Registry before calling, and (2) maintaining an internal 'company-specific' do not call list of individuals who have personally requested not to be called. When someone says 'don't call me again,' the agent must add them to the internal list and honor the request — even if they are not on the national registry. The internal list must be maintained and honored for at least five years. Failure to maintain this list is a separate violation.
